did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used YOLOv12, a deep learning-based instance segmentation architecture, to perform automated multi-class nail disease detection and segmentation. The study trained the model on a dataset of 383 nail images across six classes (including Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Clubbing, etc.) to achieve pixel-level delineation and classification of diseased nail regions, reporting metrics such as mAP@50.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a YOLOv12-based instance segmentation model for automated multi-class nail disease detection. It details the methodology, dataset (383 images), training process, and reports new quantitative results (mAP@50 metrics) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Computer Vision-Driven Automated Multi-Class Nail Disease Detection using Deep Instance Segmentation
journal: Proceedings of the 8th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS-2026)
brief_summary: This study developed a computer vision-driven automated multi-class nail disease detection system using the YOLOv12 instance segmentation architecture to enable simultaneous localization, classification, and pixel-level delineation of diseased nail regions. The model was trained and evaluated on the publicly available Nail Disease Detection Dataset from Kaggle, which comprised 383 images across six classes including Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Clubbing, and Healthy Nail. Experimental results demonstrated strong performance, achieving an overall bounding box mAP@50 of 91.8% and a mask mAP@50 of 91.1%, with particularly high accuracy for visually distinctive conditions like Healthy Nail and Acral Lentiginous Melanoma. Class-wise analysis revealed that irregular or ambiguous conditions such as Onychogryphosis presented greater segmentation challenges, recording the lowest mask mAP@50 of 74.6%. Qualitative evaluations, including confusion matrices and F1-confidence analysis, confirmed the model's reliability, interpretability, and stable convergence. The findings highlight the potential of YOLOv12-based instance segmentation as an effective AI-assisted tool for automated nail disease screening and early dermatological assessment.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes localizing diseased nail regions
all_task_types.details: The model predicts object bounding boxes for localization, evaluated using bounding box mAP@50 (91.8% overall).

all_task_types.target: Pixel-level segmentation masks of diseased nail regions
all_task_types.details: The model performs instance segmentation, predicting pixel-level masks for each detected instance, evaluated using mask mAP@50 (91.1% overall).

all_task_types.target: Discrete nail disease categories (Acral Lentiginous Melanoma, Blue Finger, Clubbing, Healthy Nail, Onychogryphosis, Pitting)
all_task_types.details: The model simultaneously classifies detected instances into one of six discrete disease categories, supported by confidence scores and confusion matrix analysis.

datasets_used.name: Nail Disease Detection Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from Kaggle, curated by Gurav (2024), comprising high-resolution nail images of healthy and pathological conditions captured under diverse imaging environments, lighting conditions, and nail orientations.

use.training: Used for model training with a 60% split of the dataset.
use.validation: Used for model validation with a 20% split of the dataset; model checkpoint selection was based on validation performance.
use.testing: Used for model evaluation with a 20% split of the dataset.
use.other: The dataset contained 405 manually annotated instances generated using Roboflow for pixel-level delineation.

statistical_methods_used.method: mAP@50 (mean Average Precision at Intersection over Union threshold of 0.50)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the overall and class-wise performance of bounding box localization and pixel-level segmentation.
To evaluate localization stringency across multiple IoU thresholds.
To analyze class-wise prediction behavior, true positive rates, and misclassification patterns.
To assess performance trade-offs across confidence thresholds and identify optimal operating points.
To evaluate detection and segmentation accuracy, including box-level and mask-level metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using deterministic metrics such as mAP@50, precision, recall, and F1 scores, along with qualitative analysis of confusion matrices and training convergence.

Nail Disease Detection Dataset.notes: The model achieved strong overall performance with stable convergence. Healthy Nail and Acral Lentiginous Melanoma showed the highest segmentation accuracy (mask mAP@50 > 96%). Onychogryphosis had the lowest performance (mask mAP@50 74.6%) due to irregular structural patterns. Blue Finger showed competitive detection but lower precision due to visual similarity with healthy nails. The aggregated F1 score peaked at approximately 0.88 at a confidence threshold near 0.42.

preprocessing_techniques: Dataset partitioning into training (60%), validation (20%), and testing (20%) subsets
Manual pixel-level annotation using Roboflow for instance segmentation preparation
conclusions: The YOLOv12-based instance segmentation framework is highly effective for automated multi-class nail disease detection, achieving strong consistency between object localization and pixel-level segmentation.

The model achieved an overall bounding box mAP@50 of 91.8% and a mask mAP@50 of 91.1%, supported by stable training convergence and minimal divergence between training and validation behaviors, indicating effective optimization without significant overfitting.

Class-wise evaluation revealed that visually distinctive conditions, specifically Healthy Nail (mask mAP@50: 98.3%) and Acral Lentiginous Melanoma (mask mAP@50: 96.5%), achieved the highest segmentation accuracy due to well-defined morphological and color features.

Structurally complex or visually ambiguous conditions, such as Onychogryphosis (mask mAP@50: 74.6%) and Blue Finger, posed greater segmentation challenges due to irregular curvature, heterogeneous appearance, and overlapping color characteristics with healthy nails.

Qualitative visualizations, F1-confidence analysis, and confusion matrix evaluation confirmed the model's robustness, generalization capability, and interpretability, with an optimal operating threshold identified for balanced precision and recall.

The findings highlight the potential of YOLOv12-based instance segmentation as a reliable and scalable AI-assisted approach for automated nail disease analysis, offering a promising foundation for early screening and supportive dermatological assessment in accessible healthcare settings.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-source dataset (383 images from the Kaggle Nail Disease Detection Dataset), which limits the generalizability of the results and raises concerns about external validation.

Performance is significantly biased by visual distinctiveness; classes with clear morphological features (Healthy Nail, Acral Lentiginous Melanoma) achieved high accuracy (mask mAP@50 > 96%), while structurally complex or ambiguous classes (Onychogryphosis, Blue Finger) showed lower performance (mask mAP@50 of 74.6% and 91.0% respectively).

The Blue Finger class exhibited lower precision (85.3%) and was susceptible to confusion with Healthy Nail due to overlapping color tones and lighting-dependent cues, indicating potential limitations in distinguishing subtle pathological variations.

Onychogryphosis presented the greatest segmentation challenge with the lowest mask mAP@50 (74.6%) and recall (73.8%), attributed to irregular curvature, variable thickness, and heterogeneous appearance, highlighting a limitation in handling complex structural deformities.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, which may have constrained the model's ability to learn robust features from the limited dataset size.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying instead on deterministic metrics (mAP@50, precision, recall) and qualitative analysis.

The dataset split (60% training, 20% validation, 20% testing) was applied to a small total sample size, potentially leading to high variance in performance estimates, particularly for minority classes.

The model was deployed via a Streamlit web application, suggesting potential for accessibility in low-resource settings, but the lack of multi-center or diverse demographic validation limits its immediate clinical applicability.
